---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
---

<!-- the section below is shown only if rendering is beamer from latex. (and format:revealjs is commmented) -->

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDES
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{5.2 - Assessing Validity: Factor Analysis}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```


## Correlation Matrix
```{r, echo=FALSE, output=TRUE, fig.width=4,fig.height=3.5, fig.align='center', out.width="70%"}
library("corrplot")

mat = matrix(c(1, -0.7, -0.6, -0.01,
               -0.7, 1, 0.5, 0.03,
               -0.6, 0.5, 1, 0.02,
               -0.01, 0.03, 0.02, 1
), byrow=TRUE, ncol=4)

colnames(mat)=rownames(mat)=c("Anxiety Scale", "Well-Being Scale", "Quality of Life Scale", "Age")


# isSymmetric.matrix(mat)
corrplot(mat, method = "number", col = "black", cl.pos = "n" )


```

## Factors
```{=latex}
\usetikzlibrary{shapes.geometric, arrows.meta}

\begin{tikzpicture}[
oval/.style={
draw,
ellipse,
minimum width=3cm,
minimum height=0.7cm,
font=\small,
align=center
},
rect/.style={
draw,
rectangle,
minimum width=2.2cm,
minimum height=0.7cm,
font=\small,
align=center
},
arr/.style={-{Stealth[length=4pt]}, gray!70!black},
node distance=0.55cm
]

%--- Right column: rectangles ---
\foreach \name [count=\i] in {Age, QoL Scale, Sociality Scale, Anxiety Scale}{
\node[rect] (R\i) at (4, {--(\i-1)}) {\name};
}

%--- Left column: ellipses ---
\node[oval, visible on=<2>] (L1) at (0,1) {  };
\node[oval, visible on=<3->] (L1) at (0,1) {Well Being};

%--- Arrows ---
\onslide<2->{
\foreach \i in {2,3,4}{
\draw[arr] (L1.east) -- (R\i.west);
}
}



\end{tikzpicture}
```

## Factors and Loadings

| Variable         | Factor 1 |
|------------------|----------|
| Anxiety Scale    | 0.72     |
| Sociality Scale  | 0.65     |
| QoL Scale        | 0.58     |
| Age              | 0.01     |
\pause
Loadings can be interpreted as the correlation between a variable and a Factor.


## Factors and items
```{=latex}
\usetikzlibrary{shapes.geometric, arrows.meta}

\begin{tikzpicture}[
oval/.style={
draw,
ellipse,
minimum width=2.2cm,
minimum height=0.7cm,
font=\small,
align=center
},
rect/.style={
draw,
rectangle,
minimum width=2.2cm,
minimum height=0.7cm,
font=\small,
align=center
},
arr/.style={-{Stealth[length=4pt]}, gray!70!black},
node distance=0.55cm
]

%--- Right column: rectangles ---
\foreach \i in {1,...,10}{
\node[rect] (R\i) at (5, {-(\i-1)*0.8}) {item\i};
}

%--- Left column: ellipses ---
\onslide<2->{
\node[oval] (L1) at (0, {-(2.5)*0.95}) {Factor 1};
\node[oval] (L2) at (0, {-(5.5)*0.95}) {Factor 2};

%--- Arrows ---
\foreach \i in {1,2,3,4,5,8}{
\draw[arr] (L1.east) -- (R\i.west);
}

\foreach \i in {6,9,10}{
\draw[arr] (L2.east) -- (R\i.west);
}
}

\end{tikzpicture}
```

## Factor loadings

| Item   | Factor 1 | Factor 2 |
|--------|----------|----------|
| Item 1 | 0.72     |          |
| Item 2 | 0.68     |          |
| Item 3 | 0.75     |          |
| Item 4 | 0.70     |          |
| Item 5 | 0.66     |          |
| Item 6 |          | 0.71     |
| Item 7 |          |          |
| Item 8 | 0.69     |          |
| Item 9 |          | 0.74     |
| Item 10|          | 0.73     |


## Factor analysis
\small

In tests developed with Classical Test Theory, Factor Analysis is often used to check if the items correlates in a meaningful way _according to the expectations_.
\pause

- **Exporatory Factor Analysis** analysis is often performed on a first step on some participants, if the grouping is not known.
\pause
- **Confirmatory Factor Analysis** is performed then (on different participants), to check if factor solution is confirmed.

## Factor analysis - recommendation

Factor analysis requires about >200 participants, with 10-20 items for participant (but see Mac Callum et al. 1999)

Results of Confirmatory Factor Analysis are assessed with different metrics of fit quality, more commonly:

- **RMSEA** (Root Mean Squared Error Approximation) should be < 0.06
- **CFI** (Confirmatory Factor Index) should be higher than 0.9
- and others ...

## Factor analysis

In the literature you may find confusion between Exploratory and Confirmatory factor analysis.

\pause

An important aspect is that if both are performed this should not be on the same sample!

## Interpreting Factor analysis

Imaging this scenario

| Item   | Factor 1 | Factor 2 |
|--------|----------|----------|
| I often feel anxious | 0.72     |          |
| My hearth races with no reason | 0.68     |          |
| I find hard to stop worrying | 0.75     |          |
| I often anticipate the wors possible outcomes | 0.70     |          |
| I feel afraid something bad may happen | 0.66     |          |

\pause
What this result tell?

\pause
<!--### INSERT QUIZ HERE --->

## Interpreting Factor analysis
![](Figures/triangle.png){width="15%" fig-align="center"}

Factor analysis _per se_ in the context of psychometrics only tells my about item correlations, but NOT
the interpretation.

\pause
Assigning a Label to a Factor is something done by the researcher/experimenter

\pause
To support construct interpretation other data are necessary (e.g. correlation of the total score of those items with another test/scale)

## Interpreting Factor analysis (example)

Is the structure of the test adherent to the theory? **OK**
\pause

Is the item grouping justified? **OK**
\pause

Are we sure we are measuring that construct (e.g. Anxiety, Memory)? **NO**
